curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 5 (Ethical and Professional Standards), Learning Module 6: Guidance for Standard V: Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions

reading.reading_name: Guidance for Standard V: Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions

drills.los_text: demonstrate a thorough knowledge of the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct by interpreting the Code and Standards in various situations involving issues of professional integrity
drills.reading_name: Guidance for Standard V: Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions

questions.stem: *Example 1 Hawke/Sarkozi (IPO shortcut)*: several oil-and-gas exploration IPOs must be priced before tax loophole closes; firm lacks sufficient resources; Hawke decides to estimate IPO prices based on relative size of each company and justify pricing later. *Most likely*:

options.A: by categorizing the issuers by general size, Hawke bypassed researching all the other relevant aspects that must be considered when pricing new issues and thus did not perform sufficient due diligence.
options.B: There is no violation because the closing tax loophole created a legitimate time constraint on the analysis.
options.C: Hawke's approach is acceptable because issuer size is the dominant factor in IPO pricing.

rationales.A:
Full statement: by categorizing the issuers by general size, Hawke bypassed researching all the other relevant aspects that must be considered when pricing new issues and thus did not perform sufficient due diligence; Hawke violated Standard V(A).

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment (Hawke): 'By categorizing the issuers by general size, Hawke bypassed researching all the other relevant aspects that must be considered when pricing new issues and thus did not perform sufficient due diligence. Hawke violated Standard V(A).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Time pressure does not lower the diligence bar; pricing by size category alone bypasses the required analysis.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Size is one input among many; reserves, management, comparables, and market conditions all bear on new-issue pricing.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Chandler/Dalton (timely updates)*: screens Dalton's US equity manager database; between database search and delivery to client, one of the 5 recommended firms loses its CIO, head of US equity research, and majority of US equity PMs (all leave to form own firm); Chandler does not revise report. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — data was current at time of screen.
options.B: Chandler failed to satisfy the requirement of Standard V(A) because she did not update her report to reflect the new information.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Update material info before delivery.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Chandler failed to satisfy the requirement of Standard V(A) because she did not update her report to reflect the new information; when material events occur between analysis and delivery, member/candidate must update.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment (Chandler): 'Chandler failed to satisfy the requirement of Standard V(A) because she did not update her report to reflect the new information.'
rationales.C: Timely updates.

questions.stem: *Example 3 Mastakis (group research)*: junior analyst writes fixed-income rate forecast; committee majority disagrees and changes report to indicate rates likely to increase more than her original prediction; Mastakis asks that her name be taken off. *Most likely*:

options.A: must dissociate.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: generally, analysts must write research reports that reflect their own opinion.

rationales.A: Reasonable basis → no need to dissociate.
rationales.B: Reasonable basis test.
rationales.C:
Full statement: generally, analysts must write research reports that reflect their own opinion; but results of research are not always definitive, and different people may have different opinions based on same factual evidence; when research is a group effort, however, not all members may agree with all aspects; committee may have valid reasons for issuing a report that differs from the analyst's if there is a reasonable and adequate basis for its conclusions; ultimately, members and candidates can ask to have their names removed from the report, but IF they are satisfied that the process has produced results or conclusions that have a reasonable and adequate basis, members and candidates do NOT have to dissociate from the report even when they do not agree with its contents; if Mastakis is confident in the process, she does not need to dissociate.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment (Mastakis): 'Generally, analysts must write research reports that reflect their own opinion. But the results of research are not always definitive, and different people may have different opinions based on the same factual evidence. When research is a group effort, however, not all members of the team may agree with all aspects of the report. In this case, the committee may have valid reasons for issuing a report that differs from the analyst's original research if there is a reasonable and adequate basis for its conclusions. Ultimately, members and candidates can ask to have their names removed from the report, but if they are satisfied that the process has produced results or conclusions that have a reasonable and adequate basis, members and candidates do not have to dissociate from the report even when they do not agree with its contents.'

questions.stem: *Example 4 McDermott (reliance on third-party research)*: 2-person firm subscribes to research from large investment research firm; makes investment recommendations based on these reports. *Most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates may rely on third-party research but must make reasonable and diligent efforts to determine that such research is SOUND.
options.B: Small firms are prohibited from basing recommendations on third-party research they did not produce.
options.C: Subscribing to a reputable research service by itself constitutes reasonable diligence.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates may rely on third-party research but must make reasonable and diligent efforts to determine that such research is SOUND; if McDermott undertakes due diligence efforts on a regular basis to ensure that the research produced by the large firm is objective and reasonably based, McDermott may rely on that research when making investment recommendations to clients.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment (McDermott): 'Members and candidates may rely on third-party research but must make reasonable and diligent efforts to determine that such research is sound. If McDermott undertakes due diligence efforts on a regular basis to ensure that the research produced by the large firm is objective and reasonably based, McDermott may rely on that research when making investment recommendations to clients.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reliance on third-party research is permitted with reasonable efforts to establish its soundness.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The subscription is not the diligence; periodic review of the research's assumptions, rigor, and timeliness is.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Ostrowski (subadviser selection based on lowest fees)*: uses Model RFP to source 7 candidates; feels unqualified in choosing best firm; decides to select the firm charging the lowest fees. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because Ostrowski followed a Model RFP process to source the candidates.
options.B: the selection of an external adviser or subadviser must be based on a FULL AND COMPLETE REVIEW of the adviser's services, performance history, and cost structure.
options.C: Selecting the lowest-fee adviser is presumptively prudent because it minimizes client costs.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A proper sourcing process does not cure a selection made on fees alone; the full review of services, performance, and costs is the diligence.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the selection of an external adviser or subadviser must be based on a FULL AND COMPLETE REVIEW of the adviser's services, performance history, and cost structure; in basing the decision on the fee structure ALONE, Ostrowski violated Standard V(A).

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment (Ostrowski): 'The selection of an external adviser or subadviser must be based on a full and complete review of the adviser's services, performance history, and cost structure. In basing the decision on the fee structure alone, Ostrowski violated Standard V(A).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Cost is one factor; lowest fee without evaluating services and performance history fails the reasonable-basis requirement.

questions.stem: *Example 6 Thompson (HPA scenario testing)*: analyst for subprime MBS; manager says HPA assumptions of -10%/-5%/0% are too dire because database has no negative HPA periods; Thompson insists on including this worst-case scenario, discovers securities' risk, and does NOT recommend purchase. *Most likely*:

options.A: Thompson violated V(A) by testing scenarios outside the range of the historical database.
options.B: Thompson should defer to his manager, whose seniority makes the HPA judgment his to make.
options.C: Thompson understands the limitations of his model, when combined with the limited available historical information, to accurately predict the performance of the funds if market conditions change negatively.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Testing beyond a limited historical record is diligence, not a violation — the database contained no negative-HPA periods precisely because history was short.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Seniority does not override analytical judgment about model limitations; Thompson's insistence on downside scenarios is the compliant conduct.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Thompson understands the limitations of his model, when combined with the limited available historical information, to accurately predict the performance of the funds if market conditions change negatively; Thompson's actions in running the scenario test with inputs beyond the historical trends available in the firm's databases adhere to the principles of Standard V(A).

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment (Thompson): 'Thompson understands the limitations of his model, when combined with the limited available historical information, to accurately predict the performance of the funds if market conditions change negatively. Thompson's actions in running the scenario test with inputs beyond the historical trends available in the firm's databases adhere to the principles of Standard V(A).'

questions.stem: *Example 7 Liakos/Hellenica (derivative strategy)*: derivative strategy for Carapalis (bet on rising commodity volatility); modeling group outsources components to trusted third parties; Liakos implements as firms complete; each component validated individually but strategy fails as integrated whole. *Most likely*:

options.A: Liakos violated Standard V(A).
options.B: no violation — each part validated.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Liakos violated Standard V(A); members and candidates must understand the statistical significance of the results of the models they recommend and must be able to explain them to clients; Liakos did not take adequate care to ensure a thorough review of the whole model; its components were evaluated only individually; because Carapalis clearly intended to implement the strategy as a whole rather than as separate parts, to comply with the standard, Liakos should have tested how the components of the strategy interacted in addition to how they performed individually.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment (Liakos): 'Liakos violated Standard V(A). Members and candidates must understand the statistical significance of the results of the models they recommend and must be able to explain them to clients. Liakos did not take adequate care to ensure a thorough review of the whole model; its components were evaluated only individually. Because Carapalis clearly intended to implement the strategy as a whole rather than as separate parts, to comply with the standard, Liakos should have tested how the components of the strategy interacted in addition to how they performed individually.'
rationales.B: Test as whole, not just parts.
rationales.C: Integrated testing.

questions.stem: *Example 8 Newbury (successful DD, failed investment)*: aggressive-profile client asks about Top Shelf hedge fund (20% returns for 3 years, long/short energy, extensive leverage); Newbury analyzes fund track record, principals, fees, and risk profile; recommends fund; 6 months later fund loses 60% of value and suspends operations. *Most likely*:

options.A: violation — Newbury should have foreseen.
options.B: Newbury's actions were CONSISTENT with Standard V(A).
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Reasonable basis ≠ guarantee.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Newbury's actions were CONSISTENT with Standard V(A); analysis of an investment that results in a reasonable basis for recommendation does NOT guarantee that the investment has no downside risk; Newbury must discuss the analysis process with the client while reminding the client that past performance does not lead to guaranteed future gains and that losses in an aggressive investment portfolio should be expected.

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment (Newbury): 'Newbury's actions were consistent with Standard V(A). Analysis of an investment that results in a reasonable basis for recommendation does not guarantee that the investment has no downside risk. Newbury must discuss the analysis process with the client while reminding the client that past performance does not lead to guaranteed future gains and that losses in an aggressive investment portfolio should be expected.'
rationales.C: Basis ≠ guarantee.

questions.stem: *Example 9 Cannon (blog research)*: quant analyst for CityCenter Hedge Fund; reads blog run by 'Expert CFA' with intriguing research; incorporates factors from online research under pressure to improve model; updated output recommends new investments. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because published blog research is public information available to any analyst.
options.B: Cannon's only error was failing to cite the blog, a plagiarism issue under Standard I(C).
options.C: Cannon violated Standard V(A) by failing to have a reasonable basis for the new recommendations made to the portfolio managers.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Public availability does not confer a reasonable basis; the factors were deployed without independent testing or validation.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Attribution aside, the V(A) failure is recommending on an untested, unvalidated basis.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Cannon violated Standard V(A) by failing to have a reasonable basis for the new recommendations made to the portfolio managers; he needed to diligently research the effect of incorporating the new factors before offering the output recommendations; Cannon may use the blog for ideas, but it is his responsibility to determine the effect on the firm's proprietary models.

Correct. Per Example 9 Comment (Cannon): 'Cannon violated Standard V(A) by failing to have a reasonable basis for the new recommendations made to the portfolio managers. He needed to diligently research the effect of incorporating the new factors before offering the output recommendations. Cannon may use the blog for ideas, but it is his responsibility to determine the effect on the firm's proprietary models.'

questions.stem: *Example 10 Stefansson (service provider selection)*: performance analyst asked to review 5 new performance attribution systems; recommends one based on impressive discussion at conference exhibitor booth without researching the others. *Most likely*:

options.A: the selection of a service provider requires the same rigor as investment analysis: full review of the provider's services, capabilities, and cost structure.
options.B: There is no violation because Stefansson's familiarity with the system from the conference is itself research.
options.C: Service-provider choices are administrative decisions outside the reach of Standard V(A).

rationales.A:
Full statement: the selection of a service provider requires the same rigor as investment analysis: full review of the provider's services, capabilities, and cost structure; basing recommendation on a single impressive booth discussion without comparative analysis of other options violates Standard V(A).

Correct. Per Example 10 (Stefansson): Selecting a service provider requires the same rigor as investment analysis. Members and candidates must review external providers as diligently as they review individual investment opportunities. Basing a recommendation on impressions from a booth discussion — without researching the other four options — does not meet the reasonable-basis threshold under Standard V(A).
rationales.B: Incorrect. An impressive conference discussion is not the full review of services, capabilities, and costs that diligence requires.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Selecting service providers demands the same rigor as investment analysis under the standard's guidance.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 1 Willier (overheard opinion)*: research analyst who has documented weak 'hold' outlook for Company X; overhears financial analyst from another firm at lunch offering conflicting opinions; upon returning to office, releases strong 'buy' recommendation. *Most likely*:

options.A: Violated by failing to distinguish facts from opinions.
options.B: violated the Standards because he did not have a reasonable and adequate basis for his recommendation.
options.C: In full compliance.

rationales.A: Reasonable basis issue.
rationales.B:
Full statement: violated the Standards because he did not have a reasonable and adequate basis for his recommendation; the opinion of another financial analyst is not an adequate basis for Willier's action in changing the recommendation; if the opinion overheard by Willier had sparked him to conduct additional research and investigation that justified a change of opinion, then a changed recommendation would be appropriate.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 1 Solution: 'This question relates to Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis. The opinion of another financial analyst is not an adequate basis for Willier's action in changing the recommendation. C is thus incorrect. So is Option A because, although it is true that members and candidates must distinguish between facts and opinions in recommendations, the question does not illustrate a violation of that nature. If the opinion overheard by Willier had sparked him to conduct additional research and investigation that justified a change of opinion, then a changed recommendation would be appropriate.'
rationales.C: Overheard ≠ basis.

questions.stem: *Secondary vs third-party research* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: no distinction.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: SECONDARY research is research conducted by someone else in the member's or candidate's firm.

rationales.A: Secondary = inside firm; Third-party = outside.
rationales.B: Definitions.
rationales.C:
Full statement: SECONDARY research is research conducted by someone else in the member's or candidate's firm; THIRD-PARTY research is research conducted by entities OUTSIDE the member's or candidate's firm, such as a brokerage firm, bank, or research firm; if members and candidates rely on secondary or third-party research, they must make reasonable and diligent efforts to determine that such research is sound.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Information Sources: 'Secondary research is research conducted by someone else in the member's or candidate's firm. Third-party research is research conducted by entities outside the member's or candidate's firm, such as a brokerage firm, bank, or research firm.'

questions.stem: *Biased/unreliable research source* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: if a member or candidate has reason to suspect that either secondary or third-party research or information comes from a source that may be biased, unreliable, or otherwise deficient, the member or candidate must NOT rely on that information.
options.B: A member may rely on third-party research even when its source is suspected of bias, provided the firm subscribes to it.
options.C: Suspected bias in a source matters only for sell-side research, not data services.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Information Sources: 'If a member or candidate has reason to suspect that either secondary or third-party research or information comes from a source that may be biased, unreliable, or otherwise deficient, the member or candidate must not rely on that information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reason to suspect bias, unreliability, or lack of soundness means the member must NOT rely on that information.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The soundness requirement applies to any secondary or third-party input, research or data.

questions.stem: *Good-faith reliance on firm-selected data source* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Each member must personally re-perform due diligence on every third-party source before every use.
options.B: members and candidates may rely on others in their firm to determine whether secondary or third-party research is sound and use the information in GOOD FAITH unless they have reason to question its validity or the processes and procedures used by those responsible for the research.
options.C: Good-faith reliance continues even after the member learns the firm's vetting process has lapsed.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members may rely on colleagues' soundness determinations and use the information in good faith.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates may rely on others in their firm to determine whether secondary or third-party research is sound and use the information in GOOD FAITH unless they have reason to question its validity or the processes and procedures used by those responsible for the research; for example, portfolio managers may not have a choice of which data source to use, because the firm's senior managers conducted due diligence to determine which vendor would provide information or research services; a member or candidate in this position can use the information in good faith assuming the due diligence process was deemed adequate.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance: 'Members and candidates may rely on others in their firm to determine whether secondary or third-party research is sound and use the information in good faith unless they have reason to question its validity or the processes and procedures used by those responsible for the research.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Good faith holds UNLESS the member has reason to question the process or the information — a known lapse ends it.

questions.stem: *Quant model expertise standard* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Members using quantitative models must be capable of building every technical detail of the model themselves.
options.B: Users of firm-approved models bear no responsibility for understanding how the models work.
options.C: members and candidates must UNDERSTAND the parameters used in models and quantitative research that are incorporated into their investment recommendations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The requirement is UNDERSTANDING the parameters, assumptions, and limitations — not full technical authorship capability.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Firm approval does not remove the individual duty to understand what feeds one's recommendations.
rationales.C:
Full statement: members and candidates must UNDERSTAND the parameters used in models and quantitative research that are incorporated into their investment recommendations; although they are not required to become experts in every technical aspect of the models, they must understand the assumptions and limitations inherent in any model and how the results are used in the decision-making process.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Quantitative Research and Techniques: 'Members and candidates must understand the parameters used in models and quantitative research that are incorporated into their investment recommendations. Although they are not required to become experts in every technical aspect of the models, they must understand the assumptions and limitations inherent in any model and how the results are used in the decision-making process.'

questions.stem: *Model creators vs users diligence* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: individuals who create new quantitative models and services must exhibit a HIGHER LEVEL of diligence in reviewing new products than that of the individuals who ultimately use the analytical output.
options.B: Model creators and model users owe an identical level of diligence in reviewing new products.
options.C: Model users owe greater diligence than creators, since users deploy the outputs with clients.

rationales.A:
Full statement: individuals who create new quantitative models and services must exhibit a HIGHER LEVEL of diligence in reviewing new products than that of the individuals who ultimately use the analytical output; members and candidates involved in the development and oversight of quantitatively oriented models, methods, and algorithms must understand the technical aspects of the products; a thorough testing of the model and resulting analysis must be completed PRIOR TO PRODUCT DISTRIBUTION.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Quantitative Research and Techniques: 'Individuals who create new quantitative models and services must exhibit a higher level of diligence in reviewing new products than that of the individuals who ultimately use the analytical output. Members and candidates involved in the development and oversight of quantitatively oriented models, methods, and algorithms must understand the technical aspects of the products. A thorough testing of the model and resulting analysis must be completed prior to product distribution.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Creators must exhibit a HIGHER level of diligence than users of the completed products.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The asymmetry runs the other way — the deeper duty sits with those who design and build.

questions.stem: *Data source/time horizon in models* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: no need to test beyond data.
options.B: members and candidates must also consider the source and time horizon of the data used as inputs in financial models.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Test outside databases.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates must also consider the source and time horizon of the data used as inputs in financial models; information from databases may not effectively incorporate both positive and negative market cycles; in the development of a recommendation, the member or candidate may need to test the models by using volatility and performance expectations that represent SCENARIOS OUTSIDE the observable databases.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Quantitative Research and Techniques: 'Members and candidates must also consider the source and time horizon of the data used as inputs in financial models. The information from databases may not effectively incorporate both positive and negative market cycles. In the development of a recommendation, the member or candidate may need to test the models by using volatility and performance expectations that represent scenarios outside the observable databases.'
rationales.C: Beyond database.

questions.stem: *Group research consensus* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: A member who disagrees with any aspect of a group report must dissociate from it and request name removal.
options.B: Group reports must present each member's individual view in a separate dissent section.
options.C: when research is a group effort, the conclusions or recommendations of a group report represent the consensus of the group but not necessarily the views of a member or candidate, even though the name of the member or candidate is included on the report.

rationales.A: Incorrect. If the consensus has a reasonable and adequate basis, the member need not dissociate despite personal disagreement.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The report represents group consensus; individual dissents are not required content.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when research is a group effort, the conclusions or recommendations of a group report represent the consensus of the group but not necessarily the views of a member or candidate, even though the name of the member or candidate is included on the report; in some instances, a member or candidate will not agree with the view of the group; if, however, the member or candidate believes that the consensus opinion has a reasonable and adequate basis and is independent and objective, the member or candidate does NOT need to dissociate from the report even if it does not reflect his or her opinion.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Group Research and Decision Making: 'The conclusions or recommendations of a group report represent the consensus of the group but not necessarily the views of a member or candidate, even though the name of the member or candidate is included on the report... If, however, the member or candidate believes that the consensus opinion has a reasonable and adequate basis and is independent and objective, the member or candidate does not need to dissociate from the report even if it does not reflect his or her opinion.'

questions.stem: *Example 1 ABC Capital PE Fund III (structuring fees)*: fund invests in companies; ABC Capital charges those companies for structuring advice; policy is to remit fees to fund if investment DECREASES in value but retain fees if it INCREASES; PPM does not disclose. *Most likely*:

options.A: Alphonso is in violation of Standard V(B) because the compensation received by ABC Capital has not been disclosed to clients who are investors in the fund.
options.B: There is no violation because private equity fund investors are sophisticated and can inquire about fees themselves.
options.C: Remitting fees back to the fund when investments succeed eliminates any need for disclosure.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Alphonso is in violation of Standard V(B) because the compensation received by ABC Capital has not been disclosed to clients who are investors in the fund; Alphonso may NOT rely on the sophistication of clients and their assumed understanding of the nature and details of the fee arrangements with underlying companies in which ABC Capital invests as a reason for not providing the information required by this standard; this is true EVEN THOUGH investors in ABC Capital Private Equity Fund III benefit from the arrangement.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment (ABC Capital): 'Alphonso is in violation of Standard V(B) because the compensation received by ABC Capital has not been disclosed to clients who are investors in the fund. Alphonso may not rely on the sophistication of clients and their assumed understanding of the nature and details of the fee arrangements with underlying companies in which ABC Capital invests as a reason for not providing the information required by this standard. This is true even though investors in ABC Capital Private Equity Fund III benefit from the arrangement.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Investor sophistication does not waive the duty to disclose compensation the manager receives from portfolio companies.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The remittance policy's conditionality is exactly what investors need disclosed — retention on failed deals is compensation to ABC.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Jones (manager search fee comparison)*: identifies 3 core fixed-income manager candidates; top-ranked has significantly higher fees; presents gross-of-fees performance for uniform comparison, calculates total fees for each period, lists fee schedules and additional fees. *Most likely*:

options.A: violation — fees should be primary.
options.B: Jones's presentation of manager fees is APPROPRIATE because she provided a uniform comparison of manager services.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Uniform comparison OK.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Jones's presentation of manager fees is APPROPRIATE because she provided a uniform comparison of manager services; her presentation adheres to Standard V(B), which requires disclosure about the nature of services provided, along with information about the costs associated with those services.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment (Jones): 'Jones's presentation of manager fees is appropriate because she provided a uniform comparison of manager services. Her presentation adheres to Standard V(B), which requires disclosure about the nature of services provided, along with information about the costs associated with those services.'
rationales.C: Complete fee comparison.

questions.stem: *Example 3 Maalouf (changed fee methodology)*: firm has standard method for valuing assets and calculating fees disclosed at outset; firm transitions to (1) end-of-cycle market value instead of average daily balance and (2) including cash/cash equivalents (previously excluded) — without disclosure. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — sometimes lower fees.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: advisory fees are critical information that clients need to know.

rationales.A: Methodology change requires disclosure.
rationales.B: Disclose changes.
rationales.C:
Full statement: advisory fees are critical information that clients need to know; Maalouf and his firm may change the advisory fee calculation methodology and policies over time for existing accounts, but Maalouf must make clients AWARE of any such changes; Maalouf violated Standard V(B); it is improper to change the fee calculation methodology without disclosure even if it results in lower fees.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment (Maalouf): 'Maalouf and his firm may change the advisory fee calculation methodology and policies over time for existing accounts, but Maalouf must make clients aware of any such changes. Maalouf violated Standard V(B); it is improper to change the fee calculation methodology without disclosure even if it results in lower fees.'

questions.stem: *Example 4 Williamson/Country Technicians (newsletter)*: marketing director plans exclusive investment advice newsletter; to simplify, includes only each week's top 5 'buy' and 'sell' recommendations and leaves out valuation models and portfolio structuring details. *Most likely*:

options.A: Williamson's plans for the newsletter violate Standard V(B).
options.B: no violation — simplicity needed.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Williamson's plans for the newsletter violate Standard V(B); Williamson need not describe the investment system in detail in order to implement the advice effectively, but she must inform clients of Country Technicians' basic process; without understanding the basis for a recommendation, clients cannot evaluate its limitations or its inherent risks.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment (Williamson): 'Williamson's plans for the newsletter violate Standard V(B). Williamson need not describe the investment system in detail in order to implement the advice effectively, but she must inform clients of Country Technicians' basic process. Without understanding the basis for a recommendation, clients cannot evaluate its limitations or its inherent risks.'
rationales.B: Basic process must be disclosed.
rationales.C: Basic process disclosure.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Dox/Boisy Bay Minerals (opinions as facts)*: mining analyst; completes calculation of gold reserves (500,000 ounces) based on company's latest core samples; concludes report: 'Based on the fact that the company has 500,000 ounces of gold to be mined, I recommend a strong buy.' *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — his calc.
options.B: Dox violated Standard V(B).
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Estimate ≠ fact.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Dox violated Standard V(B); his calculation of the total gold reserves for the property based on the company's recent sample drilling is a QUANTITATIVE OPINION, NOT A FACT; opinion must be distinguished from fact in research reports.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment (Dox): 'Dox violated Standard V(B). His calculation of the total gold reserves for the property based on the company's recent sample drilling is a quantitative opinion, not a fact. Opinion must be distinguished from fact in research reports.'
rationales.C: Opinion vs fact.

questions.stem: *Example 6 Thomas/Government Brokers (structured product)*: report describes strategy benefiting from expected interest rate decline; report cites proprietary nature and does not describe how firm may offer returns or the related risks; no discussion of rising-rate scenarios. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because proprietary strategies are exempt from risk-description requirements.
options.B: The report is deficient only in omitting the strategy's historical performance table.
options.C: Thomas violated Standard V(B) because her report fails to describe properly the basic characteristics of the actual and implied risks of the investment strategy, including how the structure was created and the degree to which leverage was embedded in the structure.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Proprietary protection does not excuse failing to describe the basic characteristics and implied risks of the strategy.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The failure is the missing risk and factor discussion — including how the strategy behaves if rates rise instead.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Thomas violated Standard V(B) because her report fails to describe properly the basic characteristics of the actual and implied risks of the investment strategy, including how the structure was created and the degree to which leverage was embedded in the structure; the report must include a BALANCED DISCUSSION of how the strategy would perform in the case of both rising and falling interest rates, preferably illustrating how the strategies might be expected to perform in the event of a reasonable variety of interest rate and credit risk–spread scenarios; if liquidity issues are relevant to valuation of either the derivatives or the underlying securities, Thomas must address those risks as well.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment (Thomas): 'Thomas violated Standard V(B) because her report fails to describe properly the basic characteristics of the actual and implied risks of the investment strategy, including how the structure was created and the degree to which leverage was embedded in the structure. The report must include a balanced discussion of how the strategy would perform in the case of both rising and falling interest rates, preferably illustrating how the strategies might be expected to perform in the event of a reasonable variety of interest rate and credit risk–spread scenarios.'

questions.stem: *Example 7 Clio/May & Associates (market-cap ceiling)*: aggressive growth small-cap US manager; grew AUM to $20B; CIO Clio lifts max market-cap ceiling from $2B to $8B and changes sales/marketing literature accordingly to inform PROSPECTIVE clients; but does not tell existing clients. *Most likely*:

options.A: although Clio appropriately informs potentially interested parties about the change in investment process, he must ALSO notify May's EXISTING clients.
options.B: There is no violation because Clio informed prospective clients and consultants of the new market-cap ceiling.
options.C: Raising the ceiling is a portfolio-level tactic requiring no client communication at all.

rationales.A:
Full statement: although Clio appropriately informs potentially interested parties about the change in investment process, he must ALSO notify May's EXISTING clients; among the latter group might be a number of clients who not only retained May as a small-cap manager but also retained mid-cap specialists in a multiple-manager approach; such clients may regard May's change of criteria as a style change that distorts their overall asset allocations.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment (Clio May & Associates): 'Although Clio appropriately informs potentially interested parties about the change in investment process, he must also notify May's existing clients. Among the latter group might be a number of clients who not only retained May as a small-cap manager but also retained mid-cap specialists in a multiple-manager approach. Such clients may regard May's change of criteria as a style change that distorts their overall asset allocations.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Informing prospects is not enough — EXISTING clients hired the firm for a $2B small-cap discipline and must be told of the change.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Quadrupling the investable-cap ceiling changes the investment process materially; V(B) requires client notification.

questions.stem: *Example 8 Clio (extending universe to non-US)*: also extends firm's small-cap universe to include non-US companies. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because adding non-US names expands opportunity without changing style.
options.B: Standard V(B) requires Clio to advise May's clients of this change because the firm may have been retained by some clients specifically for its prowess at investing in US small-cap stocks.
options.C: Clio need only update the firm's marketing materials to reflect the enlarged universe.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Clients may have retained May specifically for US small-cap expertise; extending the universe is a disclosable process change.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard V(B) requires Clio to advise May's clients of this change because the firm may have been retained by some clients specifically for its prowess at investing in US small-cap stocks; Clio must disclose changes in the investment process to all interested parties.

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment (Clio): 'Standard V(B) requires Clio to advise May's clients of this change because the firm may have been retained by some clients specifically for its prowess at investing in US small-cap stocks. Clio must disclose changes in the investment process to all interested parties.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Marketing updates do not discharge the duty to advise existing clients of the change.

questions.stem: *Example 9 Rodriguez/RJZ (new valuation model)*: active value-style equity manager using 4 multifactor models; new dividend discount model based on projected inflation, earnings growth, and interest rates; Rodriguez replaces simple price-to-trailing-12-month-earnings model with new model. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because adding a fifth model merely refines an already multifactor process.
options.B: Rodriguez must obtain written client consent before the new model may be used.
options.C: because the introduction of a new and different valuation model represents a MATERIAL CHANGE in the investment process, Rodriguez must communicate the change to the firm's clients.

rationales.A: Incorrect. A new and different valuation model is a MATERIAL change to the process that must be communicated.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The duty is prompt disclosure of the change, not a consent requirement.
rationales.C:
Full statement: because the introduction of a new and different valuation model represents a MATERIAL CHANGE in the investment process, Rodriguez must communicate the change to the firm's clients; RJZ is replacing a model based on HARD DATA with a new model that is at least partly dependent on the firm's forecasting skills; this is a significant change rather than a mere refinement of RJZ's process.

Correct. Per Example 9 Comment (Rodriguez): 'Because the introduction of a new and different valuation model represents a material change in the investment process, Rodriguez must communicate the change to the firm's clients. RJZ is replacing a model based on hard data with a new model that is at least partly dependent on the firm's forecasting skills. This is a significant change rather than a mere refinement of RJZ's process.'

questions.stem: *Example 10 Morales/Fundamental Asset Management (committee stock selection)*: responsibility for selecting 'approved' list stocks shifts from individual security analysts to committee (research director + 3 senior PMs); Morales believes change is not important enough to communicate to her clients. *Most likely*:

options.A: Standard V(B) requires Morales to disclose the process change to all her clients because this is a FUNDAMENTAL CHANGE to stock selection for the fund.
options.B: There is no violation because internal governance of stock selection is not client-facing.
options.C: Disclosure is needed only if the committee's picks begin to underperform the analysts' picks.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Example 10 Comment (Morales): 'Standard V(B) requires Morales to disclose the process change to all her clients because this is a fundamental change to stock selection for the fund.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Shifting selection authority from individual analysts to a committee is a fundamental process change requiring disclosure to all clients.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Disclosure is triggered by the change itself, not by subsequent performance.

questions.stem: *Example 11 Chinn/Diversified (external managers)*: investment director; Diversified decides to limit direct investment focus to large-cap and supplement small-cap and mid-cap by hiring outside fund managers; Chinn's update letter describes the planned strategy change including reasons and characteristics of external managers. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — clear letter.
options.B: Standard V(B) REQUIRES the disclosure of the investment process used to construct the portfolio of the endowment fund.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: External manager use = disclose.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Standard V(B) REQUIRES the disclosure of the investment process used to construct the portfolio of the endowment fund; changing the investment process from managing all classes of investments within the firm to the use of external managers is an example of information about the investment process that must be communicated to clients; the charity can now make a reasonable decision about whether Diversified Asset Management remains the appropriate manager for its endowment.

Correct. Per Example 11 Comment (Chinn): 'Standard V(B) requires the disclosure of the investment process used to construct the portfolio of the endowment fund. Changing the investment process from managing all classes of investments within the firm to the use of external managers is an example of information about the investment process that must be communicated to clients. The charity can now make a reasonable decision about whether Diversified Asset Management remains the appropriate manager for its endowment.'
rationales.C: External manager disclosure.

questions.stem: *Example 12 Yakovlev/Wellard/QSC (capacity limitation)*: small-cap quant strategy; $3B capital soft threshold; marketing at $100M currently; Wellard (marketing head) tells Yakovlev to focus offering material solely on track record — Yakovlev does not object because $3B is far away. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation — far from cap.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: Yakovlev and Wellard have NOT appropriately disclosed a significant limitation associated with the investment product.

rationales.A: Known cap → must disclose.
rationales.B: Capacity disclosure.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Yakovlev and Wellard have NOT appropriately disclosed a significant limitation associated with the investment product; Yakovlev believes this limitation, once reached, will materially affect the returns of the fund; although the fund is currently far from the $3 billion mark, current and prospective investors must be made AWARE of this capacity issue.

Correct. Per Example 12 Comment (Yakovlev/Wellard): 'Yakovlev and Wellard have not appropriately disclosed a significant limitation associated with the investment product. Yakovlev believes this limitation, once reached, will materially affect the returns of the fund. Although the fund is currently far from the $3 billion mark, current and prospective investors must be made aware of this capacity issue.'

questions.stem: *Example 13 Ramon/Brickell (VaR limitations)*: risk analyst assures clients that firm's process of calculating VaR at 99% confidence level, using 20-day holding period, and applying methodology based on ex ante Monte Carlo simulation is 'extremely effective'; firm has never had losses greater than predicted by this VaR analysis. *Most likely*:

options.A: Ramon has NOT sufficiently communicated the risks associated with the investment process to satisfy the requirements of Standard V(B).
options.B: There is no violation because the firm's VaR model has performed accurately in the past.
options.C: Ramon's assurance is proper because 99% confidence statistically covers virtually all outcomes.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Ramon has NOT sufficiently communicated the risks associated with the investment process to satisfy the requirements of Standard V(B); the losses predicted by a VaR analysis depend greatly on the inputs used in the model; the size and probability of losses can differ significantly from what an individual model predicts; Ramon must disclose HOW THE INPUTS WERE SELECTED and the POTENTIAL LIMITATIONS AND RISKS associated with the investment strategy.

Correct. Per Example 13 Comment (Ramon): 'Ramon has not sufficiently communicated the risks associated with the investment process to satisfy the requirements of Standard V(B). The losses predicted by a VaR analysis depend greatly on the inputs used in the model. The size and probability of losses can differ significantly from what an individual model predicts. Ramon must disclose how the inputs were selected and the potential limitations and risks associated with the investment strategy.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Past model performance does not substitute for communicating the limitations and tail risks of the VaR framework.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Blanket assurances misstate what VaR at any confidence level can promise; the risks beyond the model must be communicated.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 2 Scott/Walkton (contingent dividend)*: expects dividend increase of $1.50/share contingent on pending tax legislation; report states 'we expect Walkton's stock price to rise by at least $8.00 a share by end of year because the dividend will increase by $1.50 a share' and 'investors should expect total return of at least 15%'. *Most likely*:

options.A: Material inside information.
options.B: violated the Standards because he failed to separate opinion from fact.
options.C: Violated by basing on uncertain future government action.

rationales.A: Not MNPI.
rationales.B:
Full statement: violated the Standards because he failed to separate opinion from fact; Scott issued research report stating he expects price to rise by $8 a share 'because the dividend will increase' by $1.50/share; he made this statement knowing that the dividend will increase only IF Congress enacts certain legislation, an uncertain prospect; by stating that the dividend WILL increase, Scott failed to separate fact from opinion; the information regarding passage of legislation is not material nonpublic information because it is conjecture; it may be acceptable to base a recommendation, in part, on an expectation of future events, even though they may be uncertain.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 2 Solution: 'Scott issued a research report stating that he expects the price of Walkton Industries stock to rise by US$8 a share "because the dividend will increase" by US$1.50 per share. He made this statement knowing that the dividend will increase only if Congress enacts certain legislation, an uncertain prospect. By stating that the dividend will increase, Scott failed to separate fact from opinion. The information regarding passage of legislation is not material nonpublic information, because it is conjecture... It may be acceptable to base a recommendation, in part, on an expectation of future events, even though they may be uncertain.'
rationales.C: Uncertain events may be basis.

questions.stem: *Communication scope* under V(B) *most likely*:

options.A: V(B) obligations attach only to formal written research reports.
options.B: In-person meetings and telephone conversations fall outside the communication standard.
options.C: for the purposes of Standard V(B), communication is NOT confined to a written report of the type traditionally generated by an analyst researching a security, company, or industry.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Communication is NOT confined to traditional written reports — the standard covers any means of conveying recommendations.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Oral, electronic, and in-person communications all carry the same disclosure duties.
rationales.C:
Full statement: for the purposes of Standard V(B), communication is NOT confined to a written report of the type traditionally generated by an analyst researching a security, company, or industry; a presentation of information can be made via any means of communication, including in-person recommendations or descriptions, electronic communications, social media posts, or media broadcasts.

Correct. Per Standard V(B) Guidance: 'For the purposes of Standard V(B), communication is not confined to a written report of the type traditionally generated by an analyst researching a security, company, or industry. A presentation of information can be made via any means of communication, including in-person recommendations or descriptions, electronic communications, social media posts, or media broadcasts.'

questions.stem: *Distinguishing fact from opinion in complex quant* under V(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Standard V(B) requires that opinions be separated from facts.
options.B: no distinction needed.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Standard V(B) requires that opinions be separated from facts; violations occur when members and candidates fail to separate the past from the future by not indicating that earnings estimates, changes in the outlook for dividends, or future market price information are OPINIONS SUBJECT TO FUTURE CIRCUMSTANCES; in the case of complex quantitative analyses, members and candidates must clearly separate fact from statistical CONJECTURE and must identify the known limitations of an analysis; must use caution when promoting the perceived accuracy of any model or process because the ultimate output is merely an estimate of future results and not a certainty.

Correct. Per Standard V(B) Guidance on Distinction between Facts and Opinions: 'Standard V(B) requires that opinions be separated from facts. Violations of this standard occur when members and candidates fail to separate the past from the future by not indicating that earnings estimates, changes in the outlook for dividends, or future market price information are opinions subject to future circumstances. In the case of complex quantitative analyses, members and candidates must clearly separate fact from statistical conjecture and must identify the known limitations of an analysis.'
rationales.B: Separate + identify limits.
rationales.C: Facts vs opinions.

questions.stem: *Non-client-facing members and V(B)* *most likely*:

options.A: everyone must ensure.
options.B: generally, this standard affects only client-facing members.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Client-facing only.
rationales.B:
Full statement: generally, this standard affects only client-facing members; while CFA Institute encourages all investment professionals to help ensure that clients receive sufficient disclosures, this standard does NOT impose a duty on members and candidates who do not interact with clients to ensure that others comply with Standard V(B); as with other standards that may not be applicable to the professional responsibilities of all members and candidates (e.g., investment performance and suitability), those members and candidates who are part of a team of investment professionals providing services to clients but who do not have client-facing responsibilities need not expand their realm of responsibility to ensure appropriate disclosures to clients.

Correct. Per Standard V(B) Guidance on Manner of Disclosures: 'Generally, this standard affects only client-facing members. While CFA Institute encourages all investment professionals to help ensure that clients receive sufficient disclosures, this standard does not impose a duty on members and candidates who do not interact with clients to ensure that others comply with Standard V(B).'
rationales.C: Client-facing scope.

questions.stem: *Example 1 Lindstrom (benchmark IPS)*: client upset by negative equity returns; IPS requires benchmark-oriented approach with 35% technology sector allocation; client complains about tech allocation. *Most likely* Lindstrom must have retained:

options.A: Only executed trade tickets need be retained to answer client complaints of this kind.
options.B: Client complaints trigger record-creation duties only after litigation begins.
options.C: for Lindstrom, having appropriate records is important to show that over the past 3 years, the portion of technology stocks in the benchmark index was 35% as called for in client's IPS.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Records supporting the process — the IPS's benchmark-oriented requirement and the benchmark's own technology weight — are what answer the complaint.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The records must exist from the time of the analysis and decisions, not be assembled after a dispute.
rationales.C:
Full statement: for Lindstrom, having appropriate records is important to show that over the past 3 years, the portion of technology stocks in the benchmark index was 35% as called for in client's IPS; to comply with Standard V(C), Lindstrom would have been required to retain the client's IPS stating that the benchmark was appropriate for client's investment objectives; would also have had to keep records indicating that the investment process was explained appropriately to the client and that IPS was updated on a regular basis.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment (Lindstrom): 'For Lindstrom, having appropriate records is important to show that over the past three years, the portion of technology stocks in the benchmark index was 35%, as called for in the client's IPS. To comply with Standard V(C), Lindstrom would have been required to retain the client's IPS stating that the benchmark was appropriate for the client's investment objectives. He would also have had to keep records indicating that the investment process was explained appropriately to the client and that the IPS was updated on a regular basis.'

questions.stem: *Example 2 Young (luxury retail research process)*: writes numerous reports based on interviews with company managers/manufacturers/economists, on-site visits, customer surveys, and secondary research from analysts covering related industries. *Most likely*:

options.A: Young must DOCUMENT and KEEP COPIES of all the information that goes into his reports, including the secondary or third-party research of other analysts.
options.B: Young need only retain notes for quantitative analyses, since qualitative interviews cannot be documented.
options.C: Retention of third-party research is the third party's responsibility, not Young's.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Young must DOCUMENT and KEEP COPIES of all the information that goes into his reports, including the secondary or third-party research of other analysts; failure to maintain such files would violate Standard V(C).

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment (Young): 'Young must document and keep copies of all the information that goes into his reports, including the secondary or third-party research of other analysts. Failure to maintain such files would violate Standard V(C).'
rationales.B: Incorrect. ALL report inputs — interviews, visits, surveys, secondary research — must be documented and kept.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The analyst using third-party research must keep copies supporting his own conclusions.

questions.stem: *Example 3 Blank/Buku (records as firm property)*: develops analytical model at Buku, documenting assumptions and reasoning; hired by competitor; takes copies of records supporting his model to new firm. *Most likely*:

options.A: OK — his own work.
options.B: the records created by Blank supporting the research model he developed at Buku are the records of Buku.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Records = firm property.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the records created by Blank supporting the research model he developed at Buku are the records of Buku; taking the documents with him to his new employer without Buku's permission violates Standard V(C); to use the model in the future, Blank must recreate the records supporting his model at the new firm.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment (Blank): 'The records created by Blank supporting the research model he developed at Buku are the records of Buku. Taking the documents with him to his new employer without Buku's permission violates Standard V(C). To use the model in the future, Blank must recreate the records supporting his model at the new firm.'
rationales.C: Recreate at new firm.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 3 Cannan (files on home computer)*: worked from home on weekends and saved correspondence/completed work on home computer; released by firm; uses files to request recommendation letters from former clients and provides reports to prospective clients as examples of her abilities. *Most likely*:

options.A: No violation — files created on own time and computer.
options.B: Violated because prohibited from saving files on home computer.
options.C: Violated because she did not receive permission from her former employer to keep or use the files after her employment ended.

rationales.A: Firm property regardless of when/where.
rationales.B: Personal computer not prohibited.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Violated because she did not receive permission from her former employer to keep or use the files after her employment ended; per Standard V(C), all files created as part of a member's or candidate's professional activity are the property of the firm, even those created outside normal work hours; without permission, she should have deleted the files.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 3 Solution: 'Cannan needed the permission of her employer to maintain the files at home after her employment ended. Without that permission, she should have deleted the files. All files created as part of a member's or candidate's professional activity are the property of the firm, even those created outside normal work hours. Thus, B is incorrect. C is incorrect because the Code and Standards do not prohibit using one's personal computer to complete work for one's employer.'

questions.stem: *Records support decisions not to change position* under V(C) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates must retain records that substantiate the scope of their research and reasons for their actions or conclusions.
options.B: Record-retention duties arise only when a review results in a trade or position change.
options.C: A decision to hold requires no documentation because nothing was executed.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates must retain records that substantiate the scope of their research and reasons for their actions or conclusions; the retention requirement applies to decisions to buy or sell a security AS WELL AS to REVIEWS undertaken that do NOT lead to a change in position.

Correct. Per Standard V(C) Guidance: 'Members and candidates must retain records that substantiate the scope of their research and reasons for their actions or conclusions. The retention requirement applies to decisions to buy or sell a security as well as to reviews undertaken that do not lead to a change in position.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Records must substantiate the scope of research and the reasons for actions OR conclusions — including conclusions not to act.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The no-change decision is itself an investment conclusion that the records must support.

questions.stem: *Historical recommendations without supporting docs* under V(C) *most likely*:

options.A: Historical recommendations from a prior firm may be reused freely because the analysis was the member's own work.
options.B: members and candidates must NOT use historical recommendations or research reports created at a previous firm if the supporting documentation is unavailable.
options.C: Prior-firm research may be used if the member attests from memory that documentation once existed.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Without the supporting documentation, prior recommendations and reports may NOT be used; the records stayed with the former employer.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates must NOT use historical recommendations or research reports created at a previous firm if the supporting documentation is unavailable; for future use of any work from a previous firm for a new employer, members and candidates must RECREATE the supporting records at the new firm; however, these new records cannot be recreated from sources obtained at a previous employer without that employer's permission.

Correct. Per Standard V(C) Guidance on Records Are Property of the Firm: 'Members and candidates must not use historical recommendations or research reports created at a previous firm if the supporting documentation is unavailable. For future use of any work from a previous firm for a new employer, members and candidates must recreate the supporting records at the new firm. However, these new records cannot be recreated from sources obtained at a previous employer without that employer's permission.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Memory attestations do not replace supporting records; the basis must be re-created from new or properly obtained documentation.

questions.stem: *Recommended retention when no local rules/firm policy* under V(C) *most likely*:

options.A: Absent other guidance, records should be kept for at least one year after the recommendation.
options.B: CFA Institute defers entirely to members' judgment, recommending no default retention period.
options.C: in the absence of regulatory guidance or firm policies, CFA Institute recommends maintaining records for at least SEVEN YEARS.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The recommended default is materially longer — at least SEVEN years.
rationales.B: Incorrect. A default exists: seven years when neither regulation nor firm policy specifies.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard V(C) Guidance on Firm and Regulatory Requirements: 'In the absence of regulatory guidance or firm policies, CFA Institute recommends maintaining records for at least seven years.'

questions.stem: *Disclosure of risks — limits on knowledge* under V(B) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates must disclose significant risks KNOWN TO THEM at the time of the disclosure.
options.B: must disclose all conceivable risks.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates must disclose significant risks KNOWN TO THEM at the time of the disclosure; members and candidates cannot be expected to disclose risks they are unaware of at the time recommendations or investment actions are made; in assessing compliance with Standard V(B), it is important to establish knowledge of a purported significant risk or limitation; having no knowledge of a risk or limitation that subsequently triggers a loss may reveal a deficiency in the diligence and reasonable basis of the research of the member or candidate but may not constitute a breach of Standard V(B).

Correct. Per Standard V(B) Guidance on Identifying Risks and Limitations: 'Members and candidates must disclose significant risks known to them at the time of the disclosure. Members and candidates cannot be expected to disclose risks they are unaware of at the time recommendations or investment actions are made. In assessing compliance with Standard V(B), it is important to establish knowledge of a purported significant risk or limitation. Having no knowledge of a risk or limitation that subsequently triggers a loss may reveal a deficiency in the diligence and reasonable basis of the research of the member or candidate but may not constitute a breach of Standard V(B).'
rationales.B: Known risks only.
rationales.C: Known-to-them standard.

questions.stem: *Ongoing disclosure obligations* under V(B) *most likely*:

options.A: one-time.
options.B: the disclosure responsibilities of members and candidates under this standard are ONGOING.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Ongoing + updates.
rationales.B:
Full statement: the disclosure responsibilities of members and candidates under this standard are ONGOING; initial disclosures at the outset of the relationship alone may not meet the requirements of the standard; if the services or costs change, members and candidates must update the disclosures and provide the updated information to all affected clients and prospective clients on a TIMELY basis.

Correct. Per Standard V(B) Guidance on Disclosing Nature of Services and Information about Costs: 'The disclosure responsibilities of members and candidates under this standard are ongoing. Initial disclosures at the outset of the relationship alone may not meet the requirements of the standard. If the services or costs change, members and candidates must update the disclosures and provide the updated information to all affected clients and prospective clients on a timely basis.'
rationales.C: Ongoing updates.

questions.stem: *Scope of cost disclosures — affiliates* under V(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Cost disclosure covers only fees charged by the entity in direct contract with the client.
options.B: Affiliate and third-party charges are disclosable only if the client requests an itemization.
options.C: the scope of the disclosures is NOT limited to only those costs charged by the entity with which the client is in a direct relationship.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The scope is NOT limited to the direct-relationship entity — affiliate and related charges are included.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Disclosure is affirmative, not request-driven.
rationales.C:
Full statement: the scope of the disclosures is NOT limited to only those costs charged by the entity with which the client is in a direct relationship; members and candidates with client-facing responsibilities must provide disclosures about ALL the costs associated with the investment services and products provided to the client; these disclosures include information on costs arising from services provided by AFFILIATES, RELATED ENTITIES, or THIRD PARTIES for products and services used by members, candidates, or their firms in providing services to clients.

Correct. Per Standard V(B) Guidance: 'In addition, the scope of the disclosures is not limited to only those costs charged by the entity with which the client is in a direct relationship. Members and candidates with client-facing responsibilities must provide disclosures about all the costs associated with the investment services and products provided to the client. These disclosures include information on costs arising from services provided by affiliates, related entities, or third parties for products and services used by members, candidates, or their firms in providing services to clients.'

drills.los_text: recommend practices and procedures designed to prevent violations of the Code and Standards
drills.reading_name: Guidance for Standard V: Investment Analysis, Recommendations, and Actions

questions.stem: *Criteria for judging whether research is sound* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: criteria that a member or candidate may use in forming an opinion on whether research is sound include but are not limited to: (1) ASSUMPTIONS USED.
options.B: Soundness of research is judged exclusively by the reputation of the publishing firm.
options.C: There are no recognized criteria; soundness is left wholly to individual taste.

rationales.A:
Full statement: criteria that a member or candidate may use in forming an opinion on whether research is sound include but are not limited to: (1) ASSUMPTIONS USED; (2) RIGOR of the analysis performed; (3) DATE/TIMELINESS of the research; and (4) evaluation of the OBJECTIVITY AND INDEPENDENCE of the recommendations.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Information Sources: 'Criteria that a member or candidate may use in forming an opinion on whether research is sound include but are not limited to assumptions used, rigor of the analysis performed, date/timeliness of the research, and evaluation of the objectivity and independence of the recommendations.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reputation is not the test — the criteria include the assumptions used, rigor of analysis, timeliness, and objectivity evaluation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The guidance supplies concrete criteria, beginning with reviewing the assumptions used.

questions.stem: *Quant model validation timing* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: Model outputs may be validated after deployment, once live results accumulate.
options.B: members and candidates must make reasonable efforts to TEST THE OUTPUT of investment models and other preprogrammed analytical tools they use.
options.C: Testing duties fall only on the technology staff who coded the tool.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Reasonable efforts to TEST outputs come before reliance — live client exposure is not the test environment.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates must make reasonable efforts to TEST THE OUTPUT of investment models and other preprogrammed analytical tools they use; such validation must occur BEFORE INCORPORATING the process into their methods, models, or analyses.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Quantitative Research and Techniques: 'Members and candidates must make reasonable efforts to test the output of investment models and other preprogrammed analytical tools they use. Such validation must occur before incorporating the process into their methods, models, or analyses.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Members who USE the models must make reasonable testing efforts, not just the builders.

questions.stem: *Broad range of assumptions for models* under V(A) *most likely*:

options.A: A model is adequate if it tests the base-case scenario the member considers most probable.
options.B: Assumption ranges should exclude negative events to avoid anchoring clients on unlikely losses.
options.C: although not every model tests for every factor or outcome, members and candidates must ensure that their analyses incorporate a BROAD RANGE OF ASSUMPTIONS sufficient to capture the underlying characteristics of investments.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Analyses must incorporate a BROAD RANGE of assumptions sufficient to capture the investment's characteristics — not a single base case.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The range should include adverse and negative scenarios; excluding them is the deficiency.
rationales.C:
Full statement: although not every model tests for every factor or outcome, members and candidates must ensure that their analyses incorporate a BROAD RANGE OF ASSUMPTIONS sufficient to capture the underlying characteristics of investments; analysis that fails to consider potentially negative outcomes or levels of risk outside the norm may not accurately measure the true economic value of an investment; in reviewing computer models or the resulting output, members and candidates must include factors and assumptions that are likely to have a SUBSTANTIAL INFLUENCE on an investment's value to ensure that the model incorporates a WIDE RANGE OF POSSIBLE INPUT EXPECTATIONS, including negative market events.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Quantitative Research and Techniques: 'Although not every model tests for every factor or outcome, members and candidates must ensure that their analyses incorporate a broad range of assumptions sufficient to capture the underlying characteristics of investments. Analysis that fails to consider potentially negative outcomes or levels of risk outside the norm may not accurately measure the true economic value of an investment. In reviewing computer models or the resulting output, members and candidates must include factors and assumptions that are likely to have a substantial influence on an investment's value to ensure that the model incorporates a wide range of possible input expectations, including negative market events.'

questions.stem: *Subadviser selection criteria* under V(A) *most likely* include:

options.A: such criteria include but are not limited to: (1) reviewing the adviser's ESTABLISHED CODE OF ETHICS.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: such criteria include but are not limited to: (1) reviewing the adviser's ESTABLISHED CODE OF ETHICS; (2) understanding the adviser's COMPLIANCE AND INTERNAL CONTROL procedures; (3) assessing the QUALITY of the published RETURN information; and (4) reviewing the adviser's INVESTMENT PROCESS AND ADHERENCE to its stated strategy.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Guidance on Selecting External Advisers and Subadvisers: 'Such criteria include but are not limited to reviewing the adviser's established code of ethics, understanding the adviser's compliance and internal control procedures, assessing the quality of the published return information, and reviewing the adviser's investment process and adherence to its stated strategy.'
rationales.B: Code + compliance + returns + process/adherence.
rationales.C: Four criteria.

questions.stem: *V(A) compliance — establish written basis policies* *most likely*:

options.A: Written reasonable-basis policies are unnecessary where analysts are experienced professionals.
options.B: recommended V(A) practices include: (1) establishing policies requiring that research reports, credit ratings, and investment recommendations have a basis that can be substantiated as reasonable and adequate.
options.C: Policy coverage extends to equity research only, not credit ratings or other recommendations.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Establishing written policies requiring a documented basis is the recommended practice regardless of staff experience.
rationales.B:
Full statement: recommended V(A) practices include: (1) establishing policies requiring that research reports, credit ratings, and investment recommendations have a basis that can be substantiated as reasonable and adequate; (2) developing DETAILED, WRITTEN GUIDANCE that establishes due diligence procedures for judging whether a particular recommendation has a reasonable and adequate basis; (3) developing MEASURABLE CRITERIA for assessing quality of research, reasonableness and adequacy of the basis for any recommendation or rating, and accuracy of recommendations over time.

Correct. Per Standard V(A) Compliance Practices: 'Establish policies requiring that research reports, credit ratings, and investment recommendations have a basis that can be substantiated as reasonable and adequate. Develop detailed, written guidance that establishes the due diligence procedures for judging whether a particular recommendation has a reasonable and adequate basis.
